examples: Compensation is assessed for rights affected by implementation of a transport scheme.
Authorities calculate compensation according to statutory principles.

faqs.question: How is compensation determined?
faqs.answer: Compensation is calculated using the principles laid down in the Act.

title: Payment of compensation
chapter: Chapter VI - Special Provisions Relating to State Transport Undertakings
officialText: Provides for payment of compensation determined under the provisions of this Chapter and the manner in which such compensation shall be disbursed.
simpleExplanation: Once compensation has been determined, this section governs how and when it is paid.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Compensation awarded under a transport scheme is paid to eligible parties.
Payment is made according to the prescribed procedure.

faqs.question: Who receives compensation under this Chapter?
faqs.answer: Persons entitled to compensation under the provisions of the Act.

title: Compensation Tribunal
chapter: Chapter VI - Special Provisions Relating to State Transport Undertakings
officialText: Provides for adjudication of disputes relating to compensation under this Chapter by the competent authority or tribunal as prescribed.
simpleExplanation: Disputes regarding compensation can be decided by the designated authority or tribunal.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A dispute over compensation amount is referred for adjudication.

faqs.question: What happens if there is a dispute about compensation?
faqs.answer: The matter may be decided by the prescribed authority or tribunal.

title: Rule-making powers
chapter: Chapter VI - Special Provisions Relating to State Transport Undertakings
officialText: The appropriate Government may make rules for carrying out the provisions of this Chapter.
simpleExplanation: The Government can create detailed rules to implement and administer State Transport Undertaking schemes.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Rules prescribe procedures relating to transport schemes and compensation matters.

faqs.question: Why are rules required under this Chapter?
faqs.answer: Rules provide detailed procedures needed to implement the law effectively.

title: General provision regarding construction and maintenance of vehicles
chapter: Chapter VII - Construction, Equipment and Maintenance of Motor Vehicles
officialText: Every motor vehicle shall be constructed, equipped and maintained in accordance with the requirements prescribed by the Central Government to ensure safety, efficiency and compliance with applicable standards.
simpleExplanation: All vehicles must be built, equipped, and maintained according to safety and technical standards prescribed by law.
punishment: Non-compliance may result in penalties, suspension of registration, or prohibition on vehicle use under applicable provisions.
examples: A vehicle must have functioning brakes, lights, and safety equipment.
Manufacturers must comply with prescribed vehicle safety standards.

faqs.question: Why are vehicle construction standards important?
faqs.answer: They help ensure road safety, vehicle reliability, and environmental compliance.

faqs.question: Do these standards apply to all vehicles?
faqs.answer: Yes, subject to the classifications and requirements prescribed under law.

title: Power of Central Government to make rules
chapter: Chapter VII - Construction, Equipment and Maintenance of Motor Vehicles
officialText: The Central Government may make rules regulating the construction, equipment, maintenance, safety devices, emissions, dimensions, weight, and other technical aspects of motor vehicles.
simpleExplanation: The Central Government can prescribe detailed technical standards for vehicles operating in India.
punishment: Violation of prescribed standards may attract penalties under the Act and related rules.
examples: Rules may prescribe seat belt requirements.
Emission standards such as Bharat Stage norms are notified under government regulations.
Vehicle lighting and braking standards are prescribed through rules.

faqs.question: Who decides vehicle safety and technical standards?
faqs.answer: The Central Government prescribes these standards through rules.

faqs.question: Can the Government prescribe emission standards?
faqs.answer: Yes. Environmental and emission standards may be prescribed under this power.

title: Power of State Government to make rules
chapter: Chapter VII - Construction, Equipment and Maintenance of Motor Vehicles
officialText: The State Government may make rules, consistent with the Act and Central Government rules, regarding matters relating to construction, equipment, and maintenance of vehicles within the State.
simpleExplanation: State Governments can create additional rules for implementing vehicle safety and maintenance requirements within their jurisdiction.
punishment: Non-compliance may attract penalties under applicable state rules and the Act.
examples: A state may prescribe procedures for vehicle inspections.
Transport authorities may implement local enforcement measures for vehicle safety compliance.

faqs.question: Can states create their own vehicle safety rules?
faqs.answer: Yes, provided they are consistent with the Motor Vehicles Act and Central Government rules.

title: Limits of speed
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: No person shall drive a motor vehicle at a speed exceeding the maximum speed fixed for the vehicle or the road under the provisions of the Act and applicable rules.
simpleExplanation: Drivers must not exceed the speed limits prescribed for their vehicle or the road on which they are driving.
punishment: Overspeeding may attract penalties under the Act.
examples: A car exceeding the posted speed limit on a highway commits an offence.
A bus driver ignores speed restrictions in a city area.

faqs.question: Who decides speed limits?
faqs.answer: Speed limits are prescribed by the competent authority under the Act and related rules.

title: Limits of weight and limitations on use
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: No motor vehicle shall be driven with a weight exceeding the maximum safe laden weight or other prescribed limits.
simpleExplanation: Vehicles must not carry loads beyond their permitted weight limits.
punishment: Overloading may result in fines, penalties, or restrictions on vehicle operation.
examples: A truck carrying cargo beyond its permitted weight violates the law.
An overloaded goods vehicle is stopped by enforcement authorities.

faqs.question: Why are weight limits important?
faqs.answer: They help protect road infrastructure and improve road safety.

title: Power to have vehicle weighed
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: An authorized officer may require a vehicle to be weighed if there is reason to believe that it exceeds the prescribed weight limits.
simpleExplanation: Authorities can stop and weigh a vehicle to check whether it is overloaded.
punishment: Overloaded vehicles may face penalties and may be required to remove excess load.
examples: A truck is directed to a weighbridge for inspection.
An overloaded vehicle is prevented from proceeding until compliance.

faqs.question: Can transport officials stop my truck for weighing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Authorized officers may require weight verification.

title: Power to restrict the use of vehicles
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: The appropriate authority may prohibit or restrict the use of motor vehicles in specified areas or on specified roads in the interest of public safety, convenience, or environmental protection.
simpleExplanation: Authorities can restrict vehicle movement on certain roads or in certain areas when necessary.
punishment: Violating notified restrictions may attract penalties.
examples: Heavy vehicles are prohibited from entering city centers during peak hours.
Traffic restrictions are imposed during public events or emergencies.

faqs.question: Can roads be closed to certain vehicles?
faqs.answer: Yes. Authorities may impose restrictions for safety and traffic management.

title: Power to erect traffic signs
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: The competent authority may place, maintain, and regulate traffic signs for the guidance and control of road users.
simpleExplanation: Authorities can install traffic signs to regulate traffic and improve road safety.
punishment: Ignoring mandatory traffic signs may attract penalties under the Act.
examples: A stop sign is installed at a busy intersection.
Speed limit signs are displayed along highways.

faqs.question: Are drivers required to obey traffic signs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Traffic signs issued by competent authorities must be obeyed.

title: Parking places and halting stations
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Authorities may determine and notify parking places, halting stations, taxi stands, bus stands, and other locations where vehicles may be parked or halted.
simpleExplanation: Government authorities can designate specific places where vehicles may park or stop.
punishment: Unauthorized parking may attract penalties or towing action.
examples: A city designates a parking area for cars and motorcycles.
A bus is required to stop only at approved bus stands.

faqs.question: Can I park anywhere on a public road?
faqs.answer: No. Parking must comply with notified parking rules and restrictions.

title: Driving regulations
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: The Central Government may prescribe regulations governing the driving of motor vehicles and road user behavior in the interest of safety and orderly traffic movement.
simpleExplanation: Drivers must follow prescribed traffic and driving rules while using public roads.
punishment: Violations may attract penalties under the Act.
examples: Drivers must follow lane discipline and traffic signals.
Vehicles must comply with overtaking and turning rules.

faqs.question: What are driving regulations?
faqs.answer: They are rules governing safe and lawful driving on public roads.

title: Duty to obey traffic signs
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Every driver shall obey mandatory traffic signs, signals, road markings, and directions issued by authorized authorities.
simpleExplanation: Drivers must follow traffic signs, signals, and road markings at all times.
punishment: Failure to obey traffic signs may attract penalties under the Act.
examples: A driver ignores a stop sign and commits an offence.
A motorist drives through a red signal.

faqs.question: Are traffic signs legally enforceable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Drivers are legally required to obey them.

title: Vehicles with left-hand steering control
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: The use of vehicles with steering controls positioned on the left side may be regulated or restricted except in accordance with prescribed conditions.
simpleExplanation: Special rules apply to vehicles that have steering wheels on the left side instead of the standard right-hand side used in India.
punishment: Non-compliance may result in refusal of registration or other action under applicable laws.
examples: An imported vehicle with left-hand steering may require special approval.
Authorities verify compliance before permitting road use.

faqs.question: Can I drive a left-hand-drive vehicle in India?
faqs.answer: Only if permitted under applicable laws and regulations.

title: Signals and signalling devices
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Motor vehicles shall be equipped with prescribed signalling devices, and drivers shall use signals in the prescribed manner.
simpleExplanation: Vehicles must have functioning indicators, brake lights, and other signalling devices, and drivers must use them correctly.
punishment: Failure to use required signals may attract penalties.
examples: A driver uses turn indicators before changing lanes.
A vehicle's brake lights must function properly.

faqs.question: Are turn indicators mandatory?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vehicles must use prescribed signalling devices.

title: Leaving vehicle in dangerous position
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: No person in charge of a motor vehicle shall cause or allow the vehicle or any trailer to remain stationary in a public place in such a position, condition, or circumstance as to cause danger, obstruction, or undue inconvenience to other road users.
simpleExplanation: You must not leave a vehicle parked or stopped in a manner that creates danger or obstructs traffic.
punishment: May attract penalties under the Motor Vehicles Act and applicable traffic rules.
examples: A truck is parked in the middle of a road without warning signs.
A car is left on a blind curve, creating a hazard for approaching vehicles.

faqs.question: Can I leave my vehicle on the roadside?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only if it does not create danger, obstruction, or inconvenience to other road users.

title: Riding on running board, etc.
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: No person shall travel on the running board or on the roof of a motor vehicle or in any other dangerous position outside the vehicle.
simpleExplanation: Passengers must travel safely inside the vehicle and not on the roof, footboard, or any unsafe external position.
punishment: Violation may attract penalties under the Act.
examples: A passenger travels on the footboard of a crowded bus.
People sit on the roof of a vehicle during a journey.

faqs.question: Is travelling on a bus footboard legal?
faqs.answer: No. Travelling in dangerous positions outside a vehicle is prohibited.

title: Safety measures for drivers and passengers
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Drivers, passengers, and vehicle operators shall comply with prescribed safety measures intended to protect life and prevent accidents.
simpleExplanation: Road users must follow safety requirements established to reduce injuries and accidents.
punishment: Failure to comply may attract penalties under applicable provisions.
examples: Passengers follow safety instructions in public transport vehicles.
Vehicle operators ensure required safety equipment is available.

faqs.question: Why are safety measures important?
faqs.answer: They help reduce injuries and improve road safety for everyone.

title: Obligation of driver regarding safety
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Drivers shall take reasonable precautions and comply with prescribed safety requirements while operating motor vehicles.
simpleExplanation: Drivers have a legal responsibility to operate vehicles safely and protect passengers and other road users.
punishment: Violations may attract penalties and other legal consequences.
examples: A driver ensures that passengers board and alight safely.
A driver avoids unsafe driving practices.

faqs.question: What safety responsibilities does a driver have?
faqs.answer: Drivers must exercise due care and follow all prescribed safety requirements.

title: Inspection of vehicle involved in accident
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Authorities may inspect a motor vehicle involved in an accident to determine its condition and whether any defect contributed to the occurrence of the accident.
simpleExplanation: Vehicles involved in accidents may be examined by authorities to identify mechanical defects or safety issues.
punishment: Failure to cooperate may result in legal consequences under applicable provisions.
examples: Police inspect a vehicle's brakes after a serious accident.
Transport officials examine a bus involved in a collision.

faqs.question: Can authorities inspect my vehicle after an accident?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vehicles involved in accidents may be inspected as part of the investigation.

title: Removal of motor vehicles abandoned or left unattended on a public place
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Where a motor vehicle has been abandoned or left unattended in a public place causing obstruction, danger, or inconvenience, an authorized authority may remove or cause the vehicle to be removed.
simpleExplanation: Authorities can tow or remove abandoned vehicles and vehicles left unattended in a manner that obstructs traffic or creates a safety risk.
punishment: The owner may be liable for towing charges, storage fees, and other penalties under applicable laws.
examples: A broken-down vehicle is left unattended on a busy road for several days.
An abandoned vehicle blocks access to a public roadway.

faqs.question: Can authorities tow my vehicle?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vehicles abandoned or causing obstruction may be removed by authorized authorities.

title: Safety measures for drivers and pillion riders of two-wheeled motorcycles
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: No driver of a two-wheeled motorcycle shall carry more than one person in addition to himself on the motorcycle, and prescribed safety measures shall be observed.
simpleExplanation: A motorcycle can normally carry only one pillion rider, and safety requirements must be followed.
punishment: Violations may attract penalties under the Motor Vehicles Act.
examples: A rider carries two passengers on a motorcycle.
A motorcycle is used in violation of prescribed safety conditions.

faqs.question: How many passengers can ride on a motorcycle?
faqs.answer: Generally, only one pillion rider is permitted in addition to the rider.

title: Wearing of protective headgear
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Every person driving or riding on a motorcycle shall wear protective headgear conforming to prescribed standards, subject to exemptions provided by law.
simpleExplanation: Both the rider and pillion rider must wear a proper helmet while travelling on a motorcycle unless specifically exempted by law.
punishment: Helmet violations may attract penalties under Section 194D and related provisions.
examples: A motorcycle rider travels without wearing a helmet.
A pillion rider fails to wear protective headgear.

faqs.question: Is a helmet mandatory for both rider and pillion rider?
faqs.answer: Yes, subject to specific exemptions provided by law.

faqs.question: Can any helmet be used?
faqs.answer: The helmet should conform to prescribed safety standards.

title: Duty to produce licence and certificate of registration
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: The driver of a motor vehicle shall, on demand by an authorized officer, produce the driving licence, registration certificate, insurance certificate, permit, and other prescribed documents.
simpleExplanation: Drivers must show required vehicle documents when asked by police or other authorized officers.
punishment: Failure to produce required documents may attract penalties under the Act.
examples: A traffic police officer asks a driver to produce a driving licence.
A commercial vehicle driver is required to show permit documents.

faqs.question: Which documents should I carry while driving?
faqs.answer: Typically driving licence, registration certificate, insurance, permit (if applicable), and other prescribed documents.

title: Duty of driver to take certain precautions at unguarded railway level crossings
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Drivers approaching an unguarded railway level crossing shall stop, take reasonable precautions, and ensure that it is safe to cross before proceeding.
simpleExplanation: Drivers must exercise extra caution and ensure that no train is approaching before crossing an unguarded railway crossing.
punishment: Failure to follow required precautions may attract penalties and other legal consequences.
examples: A driver stops and checks both directions before crossing railway tracks.
A truck driver waits until it is safe to cross an unmanned railway crossing.

faqs.question: Should I stop at an unmanned railway crossing?
faqs.answer: You must take prescribed precautions and ensure it is safe before crossing.

title: Duty of driver to stop in certain cases
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: The driver of a motor vehicle shall stop the vehicle when required to do so by a police officer in uniform or by any other person authorized by the Government.
simpleExplanation: Drivers must stop their vehicles when directed by an authorized police officer or other authorized official.
punishment: Failure to stop may attract penalties and other legal action under the Act.
examples: A traffic police officer signals a vehicle to stop during a routine inspection.
A driver ignores a lawful stop signal and continues driving.

faqs.question: Do I have to stop when signaled by traffic police?
faqs.answer: Yes. Drivers are legally required to stop when directed by authorized officials.

title: Duty of owner of motor vehicle to give information
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: The owner of a motor vehicle shall, when required by an authorized authority, provide information regarding the identity and address of the person who was driving the vehicle at a specified time.
simpleExplanation: Vehicle owners must help authorities identify who was driving the vehicle when requested.
punishment: Failure to provide information may attract penalties under the Act.
examples: A vehicle is caught violating traffic rules by a camera and the owner is asked to identify the driver.
Police seek details of the person driving during an accident investigation.

faqs.question: Can authorities ask who was driving my vehicle?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vehicle owners may be required to provide such information.

title: Duty of driver in case of accident and injury to a person
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: The driver of a motor vehicle involved in an accident resulting in injury or death shall take reasonable steps to secure medical attention for the injured person and provide required information to authorities.
simpleExplanation: Drivers involved in accidents must help injured persons and cooperate with authorities.
punishment: Failure to comply may result in penalties and other legal consequences.
examples: A driver takes an injured accident victim to the nearest hospital.
A motorist reports an accident to the police.

faqs.question: What should I do after an accident?
faqs.answer: Assist injured persons, seek medical help, and provide required information to authorities.

title: Scheme for investigation of accidents
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: The appropriate Government may establish schemes and procedures for the scientific investigation and reporting of road accidents.
simpleExplanation: Governments can create systems and procedures for investigating road accidents and improving road safety.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Accident investigation teams analyze crash causes.
Authorities maintain accident databases for safety planning.

faqs.question: Why are accidents investigated?
faqs.answer: To determine causes, improve safety, and support legal proceedings.

title: Inspection of vehicle involved in accident
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: An authorized authority may inspect, examine, or test a vehicle involved in an accident for purposes connected with investigation and enforcement of the Act.
simpleExplanation: Authorities may inspect vehicles involved in accidents to determine whether mechanical defects or violations contributed to the incident.
punishment: Failure to comply with lawful inspection requirements may attract legal consequences.
examples: Inspectors examine brakes after a serious collision.
A commercial vehicle is tested for roadworthiness following an accident.

faqs.question: Can my vehicle be inspected after an accident?
faqs.answer: Yes. Authorities may inspect it as part of the investigation.

title: Power to remove vehicle
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Authorized authorities may remove or cause the removal of vehicles that obstruct traffic, create danger, or are otherwise liable to be removed under the provisions of the Act.
simpleExplanation: Authorities can tow or remove vehicles that create traffic hazards or obstruct public roads.
punishment: Vehicle owners may be responsible for removal and storage charges.
examples: A damaged vehicle blocking a road is removed by authorities.
An illegally parked vehicle is towed away.

faqs.question: Can police tow a vehicle causing obstruction?
faqs.answer: Yes. Authorities may remove vehicles creating danger or obstruction.

title: Power of State Government to make rules
chapter: Chapter VIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: The State Government may make rules for carrying out the provisions of this Chapter relating to traffic control, road safety, vehicle operation, and enforcement.
simpleExplanation: State Governments can create detailed traffic and road safety rules to implement this chapter.
punishment: Violations of rules made under this section may attract penalties under applicable provisions.
examples: A state prescribes traffic management rules for urban roads.
Special road safety regulations are issued for school zones.

faqs.question: Can states make their own traffic rules?
faqs.answer: Yes, provided they are consistent with the Motor Vehicles Act.

title: Power of Central Government to make rules
chapter: Chapter IX - Motor Vehicles Temporarily Leaving or Visiting India
officialText: The Central Government may make rules regulating motor vehicles temporarily leaving India or temporarily entering India, including conditions, documentation, permits, and compliance requirements.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Central Government to create rules for vehicles that cross India's international borders temporarily.
punishment: Violation of applicable rules may attract penalties under the Act and related regulations.
examples: An Indian vehicle temporarily enters Nepal under prescribed cross-border arrangements.
A foreign vehicle enters India temporarily with the required documents and permissions.
A commercial vehicle crosses an international border under a bilateral transport agreement.

faqs.question: Can an Indian vehicle be driven in another country?
faqs.answer: Yes, subject to applicable international agreements and government rules.

faqs.question: Can foreign vehicles enter India?
faqs.answer: Yes, if they comply with the prescribed legal requirements.

title: Liability to pay compensation on the principle of no-fault
chapter: Chapter X - Liability Without Fault in Certain Cases
officialText: Where death or permanent disablement results from a motor vehicle accident, compensation shall be payable on the principle of no-fault liability, without the claimant being required to prove negligence or wrongful act.
simpleExplanation: Victims of certain motor vehicle accidents can receive compensation without proving who caused the accident.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A pedestrian is seriously injured in a road accident and becomes entitled to no-fault compensation.
The family of a deceased accident victim claims compensation without proving negligence.

faqs.question: Do I need to prove fault to receive compensation under this section?
faqs.answer: No. Compensation is payable on a no-fault basis.

title: Provisions as to other rights to claim compensation
chapter: Chapter X - Liability Without Fault in Certain Cases
officialText: The right to claim compensation under the no-fault provisions is in addition to other rights available under law, subject to the provisions of the Act.
simpleExplanation: Receiving no-fault compensation does not automatically prevent a person from pursuing other legal remedies available under the law.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: An accident victim receives no-fault compensation and also pursues a separate compensation claim under applicable provisions.

faqs.question: Can I pursue other compensation claims?
faqs.answer: Yes, subject to the provisions of the Motor Vehicles Act.

title: Permanent disablement
chapter: Chapter X - Liability Without Fault in Certain Cases
officialText: For the purposes of this Chapter, permanent disablement includes injuries resulting in permanent impairment or loss of bodily functions as prescribed under law.
simpleExplanation: This section explains what types of serious injuries are treated as permanent disabilities for compensation purposes.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Loss of a limb due to a road accident.
Permanent loss of eyesight resulting from an accident.

faqs.question: What is considered permanent disablement?
faqs.answer: Serious injuries causing permanent impairment or loss of bodily functions.

title: Applicability to certain claims under employee compensation laws
chapter: Chapter X - Liability Without Fault in Certain Cases
officialText: The provisions of this Chapter may apply, subject to statutory requirements, to certain claims arising under employee compensation legislation involving motor vehicle accidents.
simpleExplanation: Workers injured in motor vehicle accidents during employment may benefit from these compensation provisions in certain circumstances.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A delivery driver injured during work seeks compensation under applicable laws.
An employee involved in a transport-related accident claims statutory benefits.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to employees injured during work?
faqs.answer: Yes, in certain situations involving employment-related motor vehicle accidents.

title: Overriding effect
chapter: Chapter X - Liability Without Fault in Certain Cases
officialText: The provisions of this Chapter shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent contained in any other law for the time being in force.
simpleExplanation: If another law conflicts with the no-fault compensation provisions, the provisions of this Chapter will generally prevail.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A compensation claim is processed under the no-fault provisions despite conflicting procedural requirements elsewhere.

faqs.question: What does overriding effect mean?
faqs.answer: It means the provisions of this Chapter take precedence over conflicting laws.

title: Definitions
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: This section defines key terms used in the chapter relating to motor vehicle insurance, including insurer, policy of insurance, third party, certificate of insurance, and related expressions.
simpleExplanation: This section explains the meaning of important insurance-related terms used throughout the chapter.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: An insurance company issuing a motor policy is considered an insurer.
A pedestrian injured by a vehicle may be treated as a third party.

faqs.question: Why are definitions important?
faqs.answer: They help determine how insurance provisions should be interpreted and applied.

title: Necessity for insurance against third party risk
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: No person shall use, except as a passenger, or cause or allow any other person to use a motor vehicle in a public place unless there is in force a policy of insurance complying with the requirements of this Chapter.
simpleExplanation: Every motor vehicle used on public roads must have valid third-party insurance.
punishment: Driving without insurance may attract penalties under Section 196 of the Act.
examples: A car owner purchases motor insurance before using the vehicle on public roads.
A motorcycle rider is penalized for driving without valid insurance.

faqs.question: Is motor insurance mandatory in India?
faqs.answer: Yes. At least third-party insurance is legally required.

faqs.question: Can I drive without insurance?
faqs.answer: No. Driving without mandatory insurance is an offence.

title: Requirements of policies and limits of liability
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: A policy of insurance must satisfy prescribed requirements and cover specified liabilities arising from death, bodily injury, or property damage caused by the use of a motor vehicle.
simpleExplanation: This section explains what a valid motor insurance policy must cover and the liabilities it protects against.
punishment: Policies not meeting legal requirements may not satisfy statutory obligations.
examples: A third-party insurance policy covers injuries caused to another road user.
Property damage caused by a vehicle may be covered within prescribed limits.

faqs.question: What does third-party insurance cover?
faqs.answer: It generally covers liability arising from injury, death, or property damage suffered by third parties.

title: Validity of policies issued in reciprocating countries
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: Insurance policies issued in specified reciprocating countries may be recognized in India subject to prescribed conditions and agreements.
simpleExplanation: Certain foreign insurance policies may be accepted in India if they satisfy legal requirements and international arrangements.
punishment: Non-compliant policies may not be recognized for legal purposes.
examples: A foreign vehicle enters India under an approved reciprocal arrangement.
Insurance issued in a recognized country is accepted subject to conditions.

faqs.question: Can foreign vehicle insurance be valid in India?
faqs.answer: Yes, in cases covered by reciprocal agreements and prescribed rules.

title: Duty of insurers to satisfy judgments and awards against persons insured in respect of third party risks
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: Where a judgment or award is obtained against a person insured in respect of third-party risks, the insurer shall satisfy the judgment or award subject to the provisions of the Act.
simpleExplanation: Insurance companies are generally required to pay compensation awarded to third-party victims covered under the policy.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: An insurer pays compensation awarded to a pedestrian injured in a road accident.
A tribunal directs the insurer to satisfy a compensation award.

faqs.question: Who pays compensation to accident victims?
faqs.answer: In covered cases, the insurer is generally required to satisfy the award or judgment.

faqs.question: Can an insurer refuse payment?
faqs.answer: Only in circumstances permitted by law.

title: Rights of third parties against insurers on insolvency of insured
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: Where the insured person becomes insolvent or a company is wound up, rights against the insurer in respect of third-party liabilities shall, subject to the Act, be transferred to and vest in the third party.
simpleExplanation: If a vehicle owner becomes bankrupt or a company closes down, accident victims may still claim compensation from the insurer.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A transport company goes into liquidation after an accident, but the victim can still pursue the insurer.
An insolvent vehicle owner cannot avoid third-party insurance liabilities.

faqs.question: Can a victim claim insurance if the vehicle owner becomes bankrupt?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain rights against the insurer may continue despite insolvency.

title: Duty to give information as to insurance
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: Persons having knowledge of a motor insurance policy may be required to provide information relating to the insurer, policy details, and other relevant particulars.
simpleExplanation: Authorities or claimants may request information about a vehicle's insurance policy when required for a claim or investigation.
punishment: Failure to provide information may attract legal consequences where required by law.
examples: An accident victim seeks details of the insurer covering the vehicle.
Authorities request policy information during an investigation.

faqs.question: Can insurance details be requested after an accident?
faqs.answer: Yes. Relevant insurance information may be required for claim proceedings.

title: Settlement between insurers and insured persons
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: Certain settlements between insurers and insured persons shall not prejudice the rights of third parties entitled to compensation under the Act.
simpleExplanation: Insurance companies and vehicle owners cannot privately settle matters in a way that unfairly affects accident victims' legal rights.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A settlement between an insurer and vehicle owner cannot eliminate a valid third-party claim.
An accident victim's rights remain protected despite private agreements.

faqs.question: Can a private insurance settlement affect my compensation claim?
faqs.answer: Generally, third-party rights are protected by law.

title: Transfer of certificate of insurance
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: When ownership of a motor vehicle is transferred, the certificate of insurance and policy may be deemed transferred in accordance with the provisions of the Act.
simpleExplanation: When a vehicle is sold, certain insurance rights and obligations may automatically transfer to the new owner for a limited period, subject to legal requirements.
punishment: Failure to complete required insurance formalities may affect coverage.
examples: A buyer purchases a used car and applies for transfer of the insurance policy.
Insurance records are updated following a vehicle sale.

faqs.question: Does insurance transfer automatically when I buy a vehicle?
faqs.answer: Certain statutory transfers may apply, but policy transfer formalities should be completed promptly.

title: Effect of death on certain causes of action
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: Claims and rights arising under this Chapter may continue and be enforceable in accordance with law notwithstanding the death of the insured or other relevant persons.
simpleExplanation: The death of a person involved does not necessarily end insurance-related rights and compensation claims.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Legal representatives continue an accident compensation claim after the death of the claimant.
Insurance liabilities survive despite the death of the insured person.

faqs.question: Can compensation claims continue after a person's death?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain rights and claims may continue through legal representatives.

title: Effect of certificate of insurance
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: A certificate of insurance issued in accordance with the Act serves as evidence of the existence of a valid policy of insurance covering the vehicle.
simpleExplanation: The insurance certificate proves that a vehicle has insurance coverage as required by law.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A driver produces an insurance certificate during a traffic inspection.
An accident victim verifies insurance coverage through the certificate.

faqs.question: Why is the insurance certificate important?
faqs.answer: It provides proof that the vehicle is insured under a valid policy.

title: Duty of police officer to forward accident information
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: A police officer investigating a motor vehicle accident shall forward prescribed accident information to the Claims Tribunal and other authorities in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: Police must share accident details with relevant authorities to assist compensation claims and legal proceedings.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Police submit accident reports to the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal.
Accident information is forwarded to facilitate compensation proceedings.

faqs.question: Do police reports help compensation claims?
faqs.answer: Yes. Accident information forwarded by police is often important in claim proceedings.

title: Information regarding insurance and claims
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: Relevant authorities may provide information regarding insurance coverage and claims procedures to persons entitled to seek compensation.
simpleExplanation: Victims and claimants can obtain information needed to pursue insurance and compensation claims.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A claimant receives insurer details to file a compensation claim.
Authorities provide information about claim procedures.

faqs.question: How can I find the insurer of a vehicle involved in an accident?
faqs.answer: Relevant authorities may provide insurance-related information as permitted by law.

title: Production of certain certificates and permits
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: Drivers, owners, and operators may be required to produce insurance certificates, registration documents, permits, and other prescribed records in connection with accident investigations and claims.
simpleExplanation: Relevant vehicle and insurance documents must be produced when required during accident investigations or claim proceedings.
punishment: Failure to produce required documents may attract consequences under applicable provisions.
examples: An insurer requests vehicle documents before processing a claim.
Authorities verify insurance and permit details following an accident.

faqs.question: What documents may be required after an accident?
faqs.answer: Insurance certificates, registration documents, permits, and other prescribed records.

title: Electronic monitoring and accident information reporting
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: Authorities may collect, maintain, and transmit accident-related information electronically for facilitating insurance claims, compensation proceedings, and road safety administration.
simpleExplanation: Accident information can be recorded and shared electronically to speed up insurance and compensation processes.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Accident details are uploaded to a digital reporting system.
Insurance companies access electronically transmitted accident records.

faqs.question: Why is accident information maintained electronically?
faqs.answer: To improve efficiency, transparency, and claim processing.

title: Furnishing of information and documents
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: The prescribed authority may require insurers, vehicle owners, drivers, or other persons to furnish information and documents necessary for the administration of claims and enforcement of the Act.
simpleExplanation: Authorities can ask for insurance, vehicle, or accident-related documents when processing claims or enforcing the law.
punishment: Failure to furnish required information may attract action under applicable provisions.
examples: An insurer provides policy details during a compensation claim.
A vehicle owner submits registration and insurance documents after an accident.

faqs.question: Can authorities ask for insurance records?
faqs.answer: Yes. Relevant information and documents may be required for claim or investigation purposes.

title: Compensation in case of hit and run motor accidents
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: Compensation shall be payable in accordance with the Act where death or grievous hurt results from a hit-and-run motor accident in which the offending vehicle cannot be identified.
simpleExplanation: Victims of hit-and-run accidents may receive compensation even when the vehicle responsible cannot be traced.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A pedestrian is injured by an unidentified vehicle that leaves the scene.
The family of a deceased victim receives compensation after a hit-and-run accident.

faqs.question: Can I get compensation if the vehicle is never found?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Act provides compensation for eligible hit-and-run victims.

title: Scheme for payment of compensation in hit and run cases
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: The Central Government may establish and administer a scheme for payment of compensation to victims of hit-and-run motor accidents.
simpleExplanation: The Government operates a compensation scheme to assist victims of hit-and-run accidents.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Compensation is processed under a government-administered hit-and-run scheme.
A claimant applies through the prescribed procedure.

faqs.question: Who pays hit-and-run compensation?
faqs.answer: Compensation is paid through the mechanism established under the Government scheme.

title: Refund in certain cases of compensation paid under hit and run scheme
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: Where compensation has been paid under the hit-and-run compensation scheme and additional compensation is subsequently recovered or awarded, adjustment or refund may be required in accordance with the prescribed provisions.
simpleExplanation: If compensation has already been paid under a hit-and-run scheme and further compensation is later received, adjustments may be required.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A victim later receives compensation from another legal source after receiving hit-and-run benefits.
Authorities adjust payments according to the applicable scheme.

faqs.question: Can compensation payments be adjusted later?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain adjustments may be required under the law.

title: Compensation in case of death or grievous hurt
chapter: Chapter XI - Insurance of Motor Vehicles Against Third Party Risks
officialText: Compensation shall be payable in accordance with the Act where death or grievous hurt results from a motor vehicle accident, without requiring proof of fault in the manner prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: Victims suffering grievous injuries, or families of persons who die in motor vehicle accidents, may receive statutory compensation without proving negligence.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A road accident victim suffers grievous injuries and becomes eligible for statutory compensation.
The family of a deceased victim receives compensation under the Act.

faqs.question: Do I need to prove negligence to claim compensation under this section?
faqs.answer: The provision provides compensation based on statutory criteria without requiring proof of fault.

faqs.question: What types of injuries qualify?
faqs.answer: Grievous hurt and death resulting from motor vehicle accidents.

title: Claims Tribunals
chapter: Chapter XII - Claims Tribunals
officialText: The State Government may constitute one or more Motor Accident Claims Tribunals for adjudicating upon claims for compensation in respect of accidents involving death, bodily injury to persons, or damage to property arising out of the use of motor vehicles.
simpleExplanation: State Governments can establish Motor Accident Claims Tribunals (MACTs) to decide compensation claims arising from motor vehicle accidents.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person injured in a road accident files a compensation claim before the MACT.
The family of a deceased accident victim approaches the Tribunal for compensation.

faqs.question: What is a Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT)?
faqs.answer: It is a special tribunal established to decide motor accident compensation cases.

faqs.question: What types of cases can MACT hear?
faqs.answer: Cases involving death, bodily injury, or property damage caused by motor vehicle accidents.

title: Application for Compensation
chapter: Chapter XII - Claims Tribunals
officialText: An application for compensation arising out of a motor vehicle accident may be made by the injured person, the owner of damaged property, or the legal representatives of the deceased victim before the appropriate Claims Tribunal.
simpleExplanation: Victims, property owners, or family members of deceased victims can apply to the Claims Tribunal for compensation after a motor vehicle accident.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: An injured motorcyclist files a compensation claim before MACT.
The legal heirs of a deceased pedestrian seek compensation through the Tribunal.

faqs.question: Who can file a compensation claim?
faqs.answer: The injured person, owner of damaged property, or legal representatives of a deceased victim.

faqs.question: Where can the claim be filed?
faqs.answer: Before the appropriate Motor Accident Claims Tribunal having jurisdiction.

title: Option Regarding Claims for Compensation in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XII - Claims Tribunals
officialText: Where death or bodily injury gives rise to a claim under both the Motor Vehicles Act and the Employees' Compensation Act, the claimant may claim compensation under either of the Acts but not under both.
simpleExplanation: A person eligible for compensation under both the Motor Vehicles Act and the Employees' Compensation Act must choose one law and cannot claim compensation under both.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A driver injured during employment chooses to seek compensation under the Motor Vehicles Act.
The family of a deceased employee files a claim under the Employees' Compensation Act instead of the Motor Vehicles Act.

faqs.question: Can compensation be claimed under both Acts?
faqs.answer: No. The claimant must choose one Act for compensation.

faqs.question: Why does this provision exist?
faqs.answer: To prevent double compensation for the same injury or death.

title: Award of the Claims Tribunal
chapter: Chapter XII - Claims Tribunals
officialText: After conducting an inquiry, the Claims Tribunal shall determine the amount of compensation which appears to be just and specify the person or persons to whom compensation shall be paid and the person liable to pay it.
simpleExplanation: After examining the evidence, the Tribunal decides the amount of fair compensation and identifies who must pay and who will receive it.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: The Tribunal awards compensation to an accident victim for medical expenses and loss of income.
An insurance company is directed to pay compensation to the victim's family.

faqs.question: How does the Tribunal determine compensation?
faqs.answer: It considers evidence and awards an amount that appears just and reasonable.

faqs.question: Who can be ordered to pay compensation?
faqs.answer: The insurer, vehicle owner, driver, or any other liable person.

title: Procedure and Powers of Claims Tribunal
chapter: Chapter XII - Claims Tribunals
officialText: The Claims Tribunal shall follow such summary procedure as it thinks fit and shall have all the powers of a Civil Court for taking evidence, enforcing attendance of witnesses, and compelling production of documents.
simpleExplanation: The Tribunal can conduct inquiries using a simplified procedure and has powers similar to a Civil Court to collect evidence and examine witnesses.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: The Tribunal summons witnesses to testify regarding a road accident.
The Tribunal orders production of insurance and vehicle registration documents.

faqs.question: Does the Tribunal have powers like a Civil Court?
faqs.answer: Yes. It can summon witnesses, take evidence, and require documents to be produced.

faqs.question: Why is a summary procedure used?
faqs.answer: To ensure quicker resolution of accident compensation claims.

title: Impleading Insurer in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XII - Claims Tribunals
officialText: Where the Claims Tribunal is satisfied that there is collusion between the claimant and the person against whom the claim is made, or that such person has failed to contest the claim, it may permit the insurer to contest the claim on all or any of the grounds available to the person against whom the claim has been made.
simpleExplanation: If the vehicle owner or driver does not properly defend the case, or if there appears to be collusion with the claimant, the Tribunal may allow the insurance company to fully contest the claim.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A vehicle owner deliberately avoids appearing before the Tribunal, so the insurer is allowed to contest the claim.
The Tribunal suspects collusion between the claimant and vehicle owner and grants broader rights to the insurer.

faqs.question: Can an insurer always contest a claim on all grounds?
faqs.answer: No. The Tribunal must specifically permit it under the circumstances mentioned in this section.

faqs.question: What is collusion in this context?
faqs.answer: It means secret cooperation between parties to obtain an unfair compensation award.

title: Award of Interest Where Any Claim Is Allowed
chapter: Chapter XII - Claims Tribunals
officialText: Where any Claims Tribunal allows a claim for compensation, it may direct that simple interest shall also be paid on the amount of compensation from such date as it may specify.
simpleExplanation: The Tribunal may order interest to be paid along with compensation, usually from the date of filing the claim or another specified date.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A claimant receives compensation along with interest for delays in payment.
The Tribunal orders interest from the date the claim petition was filed.

faqs.question: Is interest automatically awarded?
faqs.answer: No. The Tribunal decides whether interest should be awarded and from which date.

faqs.question: Why is interest awarded?
faqs.answer: To compensate the claimant for delays in receiving compensation.

title: Award of Compensatory Costs in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XII - Claims Tribunals
officialText: Where the Claims Tribunal is satisfied that a claim or defense is false or vexatious, it may order the party responsible to pay compensatory costs to the opposite party.
simpleExplanation: If someone files a false claim or raises a baseless defense, the Tribunal may require that person to pay costs to the other party.
punishment: Payment of compensatory costs as determined by the Tribunal.
examples: A claimant knowingly files a false accident claim and is ordered to pay costs.
An insurer raises a frivolous defense and is directed to compensate the claimant for expenses.

faqs.question: What is a vexatious claim?
faqs.answer: A claim made without merit, often intended to harass or misuse the legal process.

faqs.question: Who can be ordered to pay compensatory costs?
faqs.answer: Any party found responsible for a false or vexatious claim or defense.

title: Appeals
chapter: Chapter XII - Claims Tribunals
officialText: Subject to the provisions of this section, any person aggrieved by an award of a Claims Tribunal may prefer an appeal to the High Court within the prescribed period.
simpleExplanation: A person who is dissatisfied with a Tribunal's award may challenge it before the High Court within the time allowed by law.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: An insurance company appeals against a compensation award it considers excessive.
A claimant appeals seeking enhancement of compensation.

faqs.question: Which court hears appeals against MACT awards?
faqs.answer: The High Court having jurisdiction.

faqs.question: Can both claimants and insurers file appeals?
faqs.answer: Yes, provided they are aggrieved by the Tribunal's award.

title: Recovery of Money from Insurer as Arrears of Land Revenue
chapter: Chapter XII - Claims Tribunals
officialText: Where any amount is due from any person under an award of a Claims Tribunal, the Tribunal may issue a certificate to the Collector, who shall proceed to recover the amount as arrears of land revenue.
simpleExplanation: If compensation awarded by the Tribunal is not paid, the amount can be recovered through the Collector using revenue recovery procedures.
punishment: Recovery proceedings may be initiated for unpaid compensation.
examples: An insurer fails to pay compensation, and the Collector recovers the amount.
A vehicle owner defaults on payment and recovery proceedings are initiated.

faqs.question: What happens if compensation is not paid?
faqs.answer: The Tribunal can initiate recovery through the Collector as arrears of land revenue.

faqs.question: Who conducts the recovery process?
faqs.answer: The Collector or revenue authorities.

title: Bar on Jurisdiction of Civil Courts
chapter: Chapter XII - Claims Tribunals
officialText: Where a Claims Tribunal has been constituted for any area, no Civil Court shall have jurisdiction to entertain any question relating to claims for compensation which may be adjudicated upon by the Claims Tribunal.
simpleExplanation: When a Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT) exists for an area, compensation claims arising from motor vehicle accidents must be decided by the Tribunal and not by ordinary Civil Courts.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: An accident victim files a compensation claim before MACT instead of a Civil Court.
A Civil Court refuses to hear a motor accident compensation case because MACT has jurisdiction.

faqs.question: Can a Civil Court hear a motor accident compensation claim?
faqs.answer: No, if a Claims Tribunal has been established for that area.

faqs.question: Why are such claims handled by MACT?
faqs.answer: To provide a specialized and faster mechanism for accident compensation cases.

title: Power of State Government to Make Rules
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: The State Government may make rules for carrying into effect the provisions relating to Claims Tribunals, including procedures, forms, fees, and other matters necessary for adjudication of claims.
simpleExplanation: State Governments can create detailed rules governing how Claims Tribunals function and process compensation claims.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A State Government prescribes forms for filing compensation claims.
Rules are issued regarding Tribunal procedures and documentation.

faqs.question: Why are rules required?
faqs.answer: They help ensure uniform and efficient functioning of Claims Tribunals.

faqs.question: Who makes these rules?
faqs.answer: The respective State Government.

title: General Provision for Punishment of Offences
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever contravenes any provision of this Act or of any rule, regulation, or notification made thereunder for which no specific penalty is provided shall be liable to the prescribed punishment.
simpleExplanation: If a person violates any provision of the Motor Vehicles Act and there is no specific punishment mentioned elsewhere, this section provides a general penalty.
punishment: Fine up to ₹500 for the first offence and up to ₹1,500 for subsequent offences.
examples: A person violates a traffic-related rule for which no separate penalty exists.
A motorist breaches a procedural requirement not covered by another penal section.

faqs.question: When does Section 177 apply?
faqs.answer: When no other specific punishment is provided for a violation under the Act.

faqs.question: Is imprisonment prescribed under this section?
faqs.answer: No, it generally provides for monetary penalties.

title: Penalty for Travelling Without Pass or Ticket and for Disobedience of Certain Directions
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Any person travelling in a stage carriage without a valid pass or ticket, or failing to obey lawful directions of authorized personnel, shall be liable to punishment under this section.
simpleExplanation: Passengers who travel without a ticket or fail to follow lawful instructions in public transport vehicles may be fined.
punishment: Fine up to ₹500.
examples: A passenger boards a bus and refuses to purchase a ticket.
A passenger ignores lawful instructions issued by authorized transport staff.

faqs.question: Does this section apply only to buses?
faqs.answer: It generally applies to stage carriages and public transport services where tickets or passes are required.

faqs.question: What happens if I refuse to show my ticket?
faqs.answer: You may be treated as travelling without a valid ticket and face a penalty.

title: Disobedience of Orders, Obstruction and Refusal of Information
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever willfully disobeys any lawful order, obstructs any authorized person in the discharge of duties under this Act, or refuses to provide information lawfully demanded, shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: A person who disobeys lawful orders, obstructs transport or police officials, or refuses to provide required information can be punished.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹2,000, or both.
examples: A driver refuses to provide vehicle documents when lawfully required.
A person obstructs a transport officer conducting an inspection.

faqs.question: Can I refuse to provide information requested by an authorized officer?
faqs.answer: No. Refusal without lawful justification may attract punishment under this section.

faqs.question: What is considered obstruction?
faqs.answer: Any act that prevents or interferes with an authorized officer performing official duties.

title: Allowing Unauthorized Persons to Drive Vehicles
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever, being the owner or person in charge of a motor vehicle, causes or permits any person who does not satisfy the provisions of sections 3 or 4 to drive the vehicle shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: A vehicle owner or person in charge cannot allow someone without a valid driving licence or a person below the legal driving age to drive the vehicle.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: A car owner allows an unlicensed friend to drive the car.
A parent permits a minor child to ride a motorcycle on a public road.

faqs.question: Can I allow a learner to drive my vehicle?
faqs.answer: Only if the learner complies with the conditions of a valid learner's licence.

faqs.question: Who is punished under this section?
faqs.answer: The owner or person in charge who permitted the unauthorized driving.

title: Driving Vehicles in Contravention of Section 3 or Section 4
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever drives a motor vehicle in contravention of section 3 or section 4 shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: A person who drives without a valid driving licence or before reaching the legally permitted age commits an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: A person drives a car without ever obtaining a driving licence.
A 15-year-old rides a motorcycle on a public road.

faqs.question: Is driving without carrying a licence the same as driving without a licence?
faqs.answer: No. This section applies when a valid licence does not exist or the driver is legally disqualified.

faqs.question: Can a minor be punished under this section?
faqs.answer: Yes, driving below the prescribed age is an offence.

title: Offences Relating to Driving Licences
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever obtains or attempts to obtain a driving licence, learner's licence, conductor's licence, or any endorsement thereon by fraud, misrepresentation, or suppression of material information shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: Using false information, forged documents, or hiding important facts to obtain a licence is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.
examples: A person submits a fake age certificate to obtain a driving licence.
An applicant conceals a disqualification while applying for a licence.

faqs.question: What if I provide incorrect information by mistake?
faqs.answer: This section generally targets fraudulent or intentional misrepresentation.

faqs.question: Does it apply to learner's licences?
faqs.answer: Yes, it applies to learner's licences and driving licences.

title: Driving at Excessive Speed
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever drives a motor vehicle at a speed exceeding the maximum speed fixed under this Act or by any authority authorized under the Act shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: Driving faster than the legally prescribed speed limit is an offence.
punishment: For Light Motor Vehicles: Fine from ₹1,000 to ₹2,000. For Medium/Heavy Passenger or Goods Vehicles: Fine from ₹2,000 to ₹4,000. Subsequent offences may attract higher penalties.
examples: A car is caught travelling at 100 km/h in a 60 km/h zone.
A bus exceeds the speed limit on a city road.

faqs.question: How is speeding detected?
faqs.answer: Through speed cameras, radar devices, or enforcement officers.

faqs.question: Do different vehicles have different penalties?
faqs.answer: Yes, penalties vary depending on the category of vehicle.

title: Driving Dangerously
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever drives a motor vehicle in a manner which is dangerous to the public, having regard to all the circumstances of the case including the nature, condition and use of the place where the vehicle is driven and the amount of traffic, shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: Driving in a reckless or dangerous manner that puts people or property at risk is a serious offence.
punishment: First offence: Imprisonment up to 6 months and/or fine up to ₹5,000. Subsequent offence within 3 years: Imprisonment up to 2 years and/or fine up to ₹10,000.
examples: A driver performs dangerous zig-zag manoeuvres through heavy traffic.
A motorist races on a crowded public road.

faqs.question: What is considered dangerous driving?
faqs.answer: Any driving behavior that endangers public safety, such as reckless overtaking or street racing.

faqs.question: Is dangerous driving different from speeding?
faqs.answer: Yes. Speeding may be one factor, but dangerous driving considers the overall manner of driving.

title: Driving by a Drunken Person or by a Person Under the Influence of Drugs
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever, while driving or attempting to drive a motor vehicle, has alcohol in the blood exceeding the prescribed limit or is under the influence of drugs to such an extent as to be incapable of exercising proper control over the vehicle, shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: Driving after consuming alcohol beyond the legal limit or while impaired by drugs is a serious offence because it endangers road users.
punishment: First offence: Imprisonment up to 6 months and/or fine up to ₹10,000. Subsequent offence within 3 years: Imprisonment up to 2 years and/or fine up to ₹15,000.
examples: A driver is found with blood alcohol content above the legal limit during a police check.
A person drives while impaired by narcotic drugs and loses control of the vehicle.

faqs.question: What is the legal alcohol limit for drivers?
faqs.answer: The Act prescribes a maximum blood alcohol concentration, and exceeding it while driving is an offence.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to drug-impaired driving?
faqs.answer: Yes. It covers both alcohol intoxication and impairment caused by drugs.

title: Driving When Mentally or Physically Unfit to Drive
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever drives a motor vehicle while suffering from any disease, disability, or condition that renders him incapable of exercising proper control over the vehicle shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: A person must not drive if a medical condition or physical impairment makes it unsafe to control a vehicle.
punishment: Fine up to ₹1,000 for the first offence and up to ₹2,000 for subsequent offences.
examples: A driver experiences a medical emergency that seriously affects driving ability.
A person with a condition causing loss of consciousness continues to drive despite medical advice.

faqs.question: Does every medical condition prevent driving?
faqs.answer: No. Only conditions that significantly affect the ability to safely control a vehicle.

faqs.question: Can repeated violations attract higher penalties?
faqs.answer: Yes. Subsequent offences may result in higher fines.

title: Punishment for Offences Relating to Accident
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever fails to comply with the duties imposed by section 132, section 133, or section 134 in relation to a motor vehicle accident shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: Drivers involved in accidents must stop, provide information, assist injured persons, and report the incident as required by law. Failure to do so is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months and/or fine up to ₹5,000 for the first offence; higher penalties may apply for subsequent offences.
examples: A driver flees the scene of an accident without helping the injured person.
A vehicle owner refuses to provide information requested by authorities after an accident.

faqs.question: Must a driver stop after an accident?
faqs.answer: Yes. Drivers are legally required to stop and comply with statutory duties.

faqs.question: What duties arise after an accident?
faqs.answer: Providing assistance, sharing information, and reporting the accident where required.

title: Punishment for Abetment of Certain Offences
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever abets the commission of an offence punishable under section 183, section 184, section 185, or section 186 shall be liable to punishment as provided under this section.
simpleExplanation: A person who encourages, assists, or helps another person commit certain serious traffic offences may also be punished.
punishment: Punishment corresponding to the nature of the offence abetted, as prescribed by law.
examples: A person encourages a driver to participate in dangerous street racing.
Someone knowingly allows an intoxicated person to drive and assists in the act.

faqs.question: What does abetment mean?
faqs.answer: It means instigating, aiding, or intentionally assisting another person to commit an offence.

faqs.question: Can a passenger be punished for encouraging dangerous driving?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the passenger's conduct amounts to abetment.

title: Racing and Trials of Speed
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever takes part in or permits any race or trial of speed between motor vehicles in any public place without the written consent of the State Government shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: Conducting or participating in vehicle races on public roads without official permission is illegal.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: A group of riders organizes a motorcycle race on a public highway without permission.
A car owner allows his vehicle to be used in an unauthorized street race.

faqs.question: Are all motor races illegal?
faqs.answer: No. Races conducted with proper government authorization are permitted.

faqs.question: Why is street racing prohibited?
faqs.answer: Because it creates serious risks to participants and the public.

title: Using Vehicle in Unsafe Condition
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever drives or causes or allows to be driven in any public place a motor vehicle which is in such condition as to cause danger to the public or which does not comply with the requirements of this Act or the rules made thereunder shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: A vehicle that is mechanically unsafe or does not meet legal safety standards must not be driven on public roads.
punishment: First offence: Fine up to ₹1,500. Subsequent offence: Fine up to ₹5,000. Additional penalties may apply where defects create actual danger to public safety.
examples: A bus operates with defective brakes that endanger passengers.
A truck is driven despite serious steering defects.

faqs.question: What is considered an unsafe vehicle?
faqs.answer: A vehicle with defects that make it dangerous for road use, such as faulty brakes, steering, or lighting systems.

faqs.question: Can the owner be punished even if not driving?
faqs.answer: Yes. A person who causes or permits the use of an unsafe vehicle may also be liable.

title: Punishment for Offences Relating to Motor Vehicle Standards
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Any manufacturer, importer, dealer, or owner who violates prescribed motor vehicle standards, recalls, or compliance requirements under the Act shall be liable to punishment as specified.
simpleExplanation: Manufacturers and other responsible parties must comply with vehicle safety and environmental standards. Violations can attract significant penalties.
punishment: Penalty varies depending on the nature of the violation and may include substantial fines prescribed under the Act.
examples: A manufacturer fails to comply with mandatory vehicle recall requirements.
A vehicle is sold despite not meeting prescribed safety standards.

faqs.question: Who can be punished under this section?
faqs.answer: Manufacturers, importers, dealers, and other responsible persons involved in non-compliance.

faqs.question: Why are vehicle standards important?
faqs.answer: They ensure road safety, environmental compliance, and consumer protection.

title: Sale of Vehicle in Unsafe Condition or Contravention of Standards
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever sells, delivers, or offers for sale a motor vehicle or component in contravention of prescribed standards or in an unsafe condition shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: It is illegal to sell or supply a vehicle that does not meet required safety standards or is unsafe for road use.
punishment: Fine and other penalties as prescribed under the Act.
examples: A dealer sells a vehicle with known safety defects.
A seller offers a vehicle that does not comply with mandatory standards.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to new vehicles?
faqs.answer: It applies to vehicles and components covered by the statutory requirements.

faqs.question: Can dealers be penalized?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dealers and other responsible sellers may be held liable.

title: Using Vehicle Without Registration
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever drives or causes or allows a motor vehicle to be driven in contravention of section 39 relating to registration shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: A motor vehicle must be properly registered before it can be legally used on a public road.
punishment: First offence: Fine up to ₹5,000. Subsequent offence: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.
examples: A newly purchased car is driven on the road without valid registration.
An owner continues using a vehicle after registration has been cancelled.

faqs.question: Can I drive an unregistered vehicle on public roads?
faqs.answer: No, except where specifically permitted under the law, such as temporary registration provisions.

faqs.question: Who is liable under this section?
faqs.answer: The driver and any person who causes or permits the violation.

title: Using Vehicle Without Permit
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever drives a transport vehicle or causes or allows a transport vehicle to be used in contravention of permit conditions or without a valid permit shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: Commercial and transport vehicles must have the required permit and must operate according to permit conditions.
punishment: First offence: Fine up to ₹10,000 and/or imprisonment up to 6 months. Subsequent offences may attract higher penalties.
examples: A goods vehicle operates without a valid transport permit.
A bus runs on a route not authorized by its permit.

faqs.question: Do private vehicles need permits under this section?
faqs.answer: This section primarily applies to transport vehicles that require permits.

faqs.question: What is a permit violation?
faqs.answer: Operating without a permit or breaching the conditions of a valid permit.

title: Using Vehicle Without Fitness Certificate
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever drives or causes or permits a transport vehicle to be driven without a valid certificate of fitness as required under the Act shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: Transport vehicles such as buses, taxis, and trucks must possess a valid fitness certificate proving they are roadworthy.
punishment: Fine up to ₹10,000 for the first offence and higher penalties for subsequent offences as prescribed by law.
examples: A truck continues operating after its fitness certificate has expired.
A bus owner allows a vehicle without a valid fitness certificate to carry passengers.

faqs.question: What is a fitness certificate?
faqs.answer: It is an official document certifying that a transport vehicle is mechanically fit for road use.

faqs.question: Do private cars need a fitness certificate under this section?
faqs.answer: This section primarily applies to transport vehicles requiring fitness certification.

title: Driving Vehicle Exceeding Permissible Weight
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever drives or causes or permits a motor vehicle to be driven in violation of the maximum safe laden weight, axle weight, or gross vehicle weight prescribed under the Act shall be punishable under this section.
simpleExplanation: Vehicles must not carry more weight than legally permitted because overloading increases accident risks and damages roads.
punishment: Fine of ₹20,000 plus ₹2,000 per tonne of excess load. The vehicle may also be detained until excess load is removed.
examples: A truck carrying cargo far beyond its permitted load limit is stopped by enforcement authorities.
A transport company overloads a vehicle to reduce transportation costs.

faqs.question: Why is overloading prohibited?
faqs.answer: Overloading affects braking, vehicle stability, road safety, and infrastructure.

faqs.question: Can an overloaded vehicle continue its journey?
faqs.answer: Generally, excess load must be removed before the vehicle is allowed to proceed.

title: Failure to Comply with Standards for Road Safety Measures
chapter: Chapter XIII - Control of Traffic
officialText: Whoever fails to comply with prescribed road safety measures or standards under the Act shall be liable to the penalties specified therein.
simpleExplanation: Vehicle operators and responsible persons must follow mandated road safety requirements and standards.
punishment: Penalty as prescribed under the Act and relevant rules.
examples: A transport operator ignores mandatory safety equipment requirements.
A commercial vehicle is operated without complying with prescribed safety standards.

faqs.question: What are road safety measures?
